title: Cable basics: c4f and c4b
subtitle: The two four-stitch cables every traditional Aran is built from.
excerpt: A cable is a column of stocking stitch that crosses itself every few rows. The two four-stitch cables, c4f and c4b, are the foundation: c4f leans left, c4b leans right. Learn the cable needle, learn the two crosses, and the whole tradition opens up.
sourceNotes:
Drawn from two public-domain references and one out-of-UK-copyright reference:

• Mary Thomas's Knitting Book (1938), §Cables — Thomas's framing of cables as 'travelling stitches' that change place across the row is the framing this entry follows. Thomas died in 1948; in the UK and EU her work passed out of copyright in 2019.
• Weldon's Practical Knitting, later series (1890s onward), Internet Archive scans — the c4f and c4b structure is reproduced across dozens of Weldon's pattern volumes under names like 'plait stitch' and 'cable stitch'.
• Public-domain Aran knitting tradition; the cable vocabulary in the National Library of Ireland's pre-1928 pattern collections is the underlying tradition the entry honours.

glossaryTerms.definition: A short third needle, often U-shaped, used to hold stitches off the work briefly while the next stitches are knitted. The held stitches then come back onto the working needle and are knitted in their new position.
The side of the knitted fabric that faces outward on the finished piece. Pattern instructions mark it as (RS); cables are almost always crossed on RS rows.
Knit on the right side, purl on the wrong side. The smooth-V fabric that cables are usually worked over. US: stockinette.

content.text: A cable is a column of
stocking stitch

(US: stockinette) that crosses itself every few rows. The crossing is what gives Aran jumpers, fisherman's scarves, and traditional Irish cardigans their lifted, ropey columns. Four-stitch cables — abbreviated
c4f
and
c4b
— are the most common cable size and the entry point to the whole tradition. The number is the total stitches involved in the crossing; the letter names which way the cable leans.

This entry covers both four-stitch cables, the cable needle they're worked over, and how to read the chart that turns up in cabled patterns. The two cables are simple to work once you've seen them done; the trick is keeping track of the row count between crossings.
What you need
A small ball of aran-weight yarn in a smooth, light-coloured fibre — the cable's twist is hardest to see in dark or fluffy yarn. A pair of 5 mm (UK 6 / US 8) straight needles. A
cable needle

in a comparable size; the short J-hook style is the easiest to handle while you're learning. Cast on 14 stitches: 4 stitches of stocking stitch, 4 stitches of cable, 4 stitches of stocking stitch, with one stitch on either edge to keep the cable from rolling. This sample is enough to show the cable's structure.
How to work c4f

Cable 4 front (c4f) is the left-leaning cable. Work to the four stitches that will become the cable. Slip the first two of those stitches onto the cable needle and hold them in front of the work. Knit the next two stitches from the left needle as normal. Then knit the two stitches from the cable needle. The first pair has crossed over the second pair, leaning to the left.

The held stitches sit in front of the work, not behind. That's the only thing distinguishing c4f from c4b. The cable needle hangs from the front of the work while you knit the next two from the left needle, then comes back up and you knit from it. With practice the whole crossing takes about ten seconds.
How to work c4b

Cable 4 back (c4b) is the right-leaning cable, the mirror of c4f. Work to the four stitches. Slip the first two onto the cable needle and hold them at the back of the work. Knit the next two from the left needle. Knit the two from the cable needle. The first pair has crossed under the second pair, leaning to the right.

The held stitches sit behind the work. Many cabled patterns alternate c4f and c4b in symmetric columns — one cable leaning left, one cable leaning right — and the two together produce the braided look of traditional Aran knitting.
Reading the chart

Pattern bodies usually pair the written instructions with a chart for cables. The chart below shows a single c4f repeat over 4 rows and 6 stitches: 1 stitch of stocking stitch on either side of a 4-stitch cable column. Knitting charts read bottom-to-top (row 1 is the bottom row) and
right side
rows read right-to-left (the first stitch in the row is the rightmost cell on the chart; the renderer mirrors automatically). Wrong-side rows reverse.

definition.title: c4f single repeat — 4 rows × 6 stitches
definition.caption: Row 1 (bottom) is RS; row 3 carries the c4f cross. The shaded purl rows (rows 2 and 4) are wrong-side rows worked straight.

content.text: The same chart applied to c4b looks identical in layout — same grid, same outer knit stitches — but the cable cell carries the c4b symbol instead of c4f. Most pattern books distinguish the two with a slanted symbol or with the labels c4f / c4b written in the legend. Read the legend before you read the cells.

definition.title: c4b single repeat — 4 rows × 6 stitches
definition.caption: Same grid as c4f; the only difference is whether the cable needle is held in front (c4f) or at the back (c4b).

content.text: Common mistakes

attrs.heading: What goes wrong, and why
attrs.intro: Cables are forgiving once you've made one — but the first three or four are usually littered with the same handful of snags.

items.symptom: The cable leans the wrong way
items.cause: The cable needle is being held at the back when the pattern asked for the front (or vice versa). c4f = front; c4b = back.
items.fix: Drop the work mid-row and check the cable needle's position before you knit the next two. If the cable's already crossed, either tink (un-knit) back to the crossing or note it as a one-off and carry on with the rest leaning the right way.

items.symptom: The stitches at the crossing are loose
items.cause: Cable crossings borrow yarn from the surrounding stitches; the first stitch after the crossing often loosens.
items.fix: Pull the first stitch after the crossing one tug tighter than usual. The next row's knit-in-pattern absorbs the slack. Blocking the finished piece evens out any remaining looseness.

items.cause: Most cabled patterns cross every 4, 6, or 8 rows. Losing count between crossings means missing or doubling a cross.
items.fix: Use a row counter or place a removable marker on the work at each crossing — the count between markers should match the pattern's row repeat.

items.symptom: The cable column looks shorter than the plain columns either side
items.cause: Cables pull the fabric in slightly because the crossing borrows yarn vertically. This is normal — it's why cabled jumpers need a wider chest measurement than plain stocking-stitch jumpers to fit the same body.
items.fix: If you're swatching for a pattern with cables, measure gauge over the cabled fabric, not the plain stocking stitch — the pattern's stitch count assumes the cabled gauge.

content.text: Where you'll see these next

The cabled dishcloth is the natural next project — a single c4f column across a 24 cm × 24 cm square of stocking stitch with garter borders, working a single cable repeat about ten times. After that, a cabled scarf with c4f and c4b in alternating columns produces the braided look. Aran jumpers ladder up from there, combining c4f, c4b, six-stitch cables, and bobbles into the dense pattern fields that give the tradition its name.
